ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Kalakand. A0CKM (milk-based dessert) is the closest defensible anchor. Exact code unavailable as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Kalakand is one square or rectangular piece weighing approximately 50 grams, providing around 183 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Kalakand is not listed as a standalone entry in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for concentrated milk sweets (khoa/mawa-based confections) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for milk-based Indian sweets. Macros and micronutrients are consistent with the known composition of full-cream milk reduced to approximately 30–35% solids with added sugar at ~35–40% of final product weight. All values are per 100g of finished product.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Milk-based Indian sweets analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kalakand found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated by analogy with other milk-based Indian sweets (e.g., burfi, peda) which have reported GI values in the range of 55–70 in peer-reviewed literature. The significant added sugar (~38g/100g) elevates GI, while milk proteins and fats provide partial moderation. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 21.0) / 100 = 13.65, rounded to 13.7. This is classified as medium GI (56–69 range). Confidence: 0.7 (estimated).

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat and caloric density; low sodium but significant dairy fat load
hypertension.guidance: Kalakand is low in sodium (50 mg/100g) and provides beneficial calcium and potassium, but its high saturated fat content (~11.5g/100g) and caloric density (365 kcal/100g) require portion control in individuals managing hypertension; limit to one small piece (50g) occasionally.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar content (38g/100g) and medium-to-high glycemic index (~65) causing significant postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kalakand is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high added sugar content (38g/100g) and estimated GI of 65; if consumed at all, restrict to a single small piece (25g) as an infrequent treat and monitor blood glucose response carefully.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5g/100g), cholesterol (~55mg/100g), and caloric density contributing to atherogenic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Kalakand consumption due to its high saturated fat content (~11.5g/100g) and cholesterol (~55mg/100g), which can adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels; occasional consumption of a small portion (50g) is permissible within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

lactose_intolerance.key_factor: Made entirely from full-cream milk; contains significant lactose
lactose_intolerance.guidance: Kalakand is unsuitable for individuals with lactose intolerance as it is made entirely from full-cream milk and contains significant lactose; lactase enzyme supplements may partially mitigate symptoms but avoidance is recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar and dairy fat may promote pro-inflammatory pathways in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Kalakand due to its high refined sugar content (38g/100g) and saturated dairy fat, which may exacerbate inflammatory responses; consume only occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kalakand is a traditional North Indian milk-based sweet (mithai) most famously associated with Alwar in Rajasthan, India. It is prepared by slowly reducing full-cream milk over heat until thick, then introducing an acidic coagulant such as citric acid or alum to create soft, grainy milk solids known as danedaar chenna. These solids are combined with sugar and simmered further until the mixture thickens and sets. The finished product is flavored with green cardamom and garnished with slivered pistachios, then cut into squares or rectangles. Kalakand has a distinctive moist, crumbly, and melt-in-the-mouth texture that sets it apart from drier milk sweets like peda or burfi. Its flavor is intensely dairy-forward with a clean sweetness. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 365 kcal, 9.8g protein, 18.5g fat (of which ~11.5g is saturated), and 42g carbohydrates with 38g sugar. It is an excellent source of calcium (420mg/100g), potassium (280mg/100g), and vitamin B12 (1.5mcg/100g). Its estimated glycemic index of ~65 (medium-high) and very high sugar content make it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. It is best enjoyed as an occasional festive treat during celebrations such as Diwali, Janmashtami, and weddings. Kalakand is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy and tree nuts (pistachios).
meal_context: festive sweet during Diwali and Janmashtami

confidence_reason: Kalakand is not listed as a standalone entry in ICMR-NIN published tables or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for khoa/mawa-based milk sweets and are consistent with the known composition of the dish. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature on similar Indian milk sweets. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for concentrated milk-based Indian sweets (khoa/mawa confections) as no standalone Kalakand entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Values are cross-validated against known ingredient composition. 2. Saturated fat (11.5g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.8g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6g), trans_fat_g (0.3g), cholesterol_mg (55mg), and water_g (25g) are estimated from full-cream milk fat composition data in ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy; these are not directly reported for Kalakand in any approved source and are marked as adapted estimates. 3. Dietary fibre is 0.0g — confirmed naturally zero as Kalakand contains no plant fibre sources; all ingredients are dairy and refined sugar. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed GI data for similar milk-based Indian sweets; no direct GI measurement for Kalakand exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 5. Glycemic load per serving (50g) = (65 × 21.0) / 100 = 13.7 (medium GL). 6. Input record contained four duplicate portion entries; deduplicated to a single 50g serving. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code for Kalakand exists in either system as of 2026. 8. The input listed gi_level as 'high'; corrected to 'medium' per standard GI classification (GI 56–69 = medium). 9. Bengali alias কালাকাঁদ and Marathi alias कलाकंद (same script as Hindi) are preserved from input and included in aliases array.
